title: Allhomes data extraction brief
text: allhomes R package extracts historical sales data Australian property website Allhomes.com.au. Starting version 0.4.0, package uses Allhomes’ Apollo GraphQL API instead scraping static HTML pages, Allhomes shifted dynamic data loading. approach queries /graphql endpoint structured JSON responses, improving data access. package relies httr2 HTTP requests tidyverse data manipulation.

title: Allhomes data extraction brief
text: Data extraction involves sending GraphQL query Allhomes’ API. Key inputs include suburb slug (specific format like “suburb-state-postcode”), year (optional), pagination parameters. API returns sales records JSON, package processes tidy data frame. Users interact via get_past_sales_data(), handles query formatting internally.

title: Allhomes data extraction brief
text: Persisted queries: package uses predefined GraphQL query identified SHA256 hash. hash ensures server recognises query can change server-side, potentially breaking requests. Variables: Queries include locality (suburb slug type), filters (e.g., minimum bedrooms/bathrooms), duration (specific year history), sorting (sale age), pagination (page number size). Request structure: GET request /graphql operation name, JSON-encoded variables, extensions (including hash). Headers specify operation proper routing.

title: Allhomes data extraction brief
text: Reliability depends SHA256 hash remaining unchanged; Allhomes updates server-side, requests fail, requiring package updates. Data fetched single request default limit 5000 records, may truncate results high-volume suburbs. Users can increase limit via get_past_sales_data(), function warns records match limit. Updates via pull requests welcome address issues.

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: goal case study show use allhomes pull past sales data various ACT suburbs fit mixed-effect model using Stan predict unimproved value (UV, .e. value plot land ) function block size (square-meters), year sale suburb. explore output model interpret results. Taking inspiration examples & modelling discussions given Gelman, Hill Vehtari, Regression Stories, case study also serves example fit mixed-effect model real-world data interpret results; become clear, model fit particularly good sense lot observed variability remains unexplained model’s input variables. However, case study useful context exploring model results different aspects model fit, represents kind analysis one might perform exploring new real-world dataset.

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: load necessary non-base R libraries, optimise use multiple cores running rstanarm model, define colour palette based nice wesanderson package.
code: library(allhomes) library(tidyverse) library(rstanarm) library(bayesplot) library(tidybayes) library(modelr) library(wesanderson) library(plotly) options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores()) pal <- wes_palette("GrandBudapest1", n = 3)

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: get past sales suburbs ACT’s Woden Valley last 10 years. easily done using allhomes::divisions_ACT dataset, filter divisions (.e. suburbs) based corresponding SA3 regions.
code: suburbs <- divisions_ACT |>         filter(sa3_name_2016 == "Woden Valley") |>         unite(suburb, division, state, sep = ", ") |>         pull(suburb) data <- suburbs |>      map_dfr(function(burb)          map_dfr(2011L:2020L, function(yr) get_past_sales_data(burb, yr))) #> Warning in get_past_sales_data(burb, yr): No sales data for suburb 'O'Malley, #> ACT' #> Warning in title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: Since want build predictive model allows us estimate residential unimproved value (UV) property based block size location, keep records data UV block size (.e. omit records fields NA) limit block sizes less 2000 sqm UVs less 2 million dollars (exclude large commercial purchases).
code: data_model <- data |>     mutate(year = as.integer(year(contract_date))) |>     select(division, unimproved_value, block_size, year) |>     filter(         if_all(everything(), ~ !is.na(.x) & .x > 0),         block_size < 2000, unimproved_value < 2e6) %>%     # Variable transformations     mutate(         # Year since 2019         year_since = year - 2019L,         # log-transformed UV         log_UV = log(unimproved_value),         # log-transformed block_size, standardised to the log of median value         log_block_size_std = log(block_size) - log(850)) data_model #> # A tibble: 3,439 × 7 #>    division unimproved_value block_size  year year_since log_UV #>    <chr>               <int>      <int> <int>      <int>  <dbl> #>  1 Chifley            360000        853  2011         -8   12.8 #>  2 Chifley            444000       1048  2011         -8   13.0 #>  3 Chifley            359000        695  2011         -8   12.8 #>  4 Chifley            341000        822  2011         -8   12.7 #>  5 Chifley            451000        760  2011         -8   13.0 #>  6 Chifley            410000        845  2011         -8   12.9 #>  7 Chifley            450000       1330  2011         -8   13.0 #>  8 Chifley            382000        857  2011         -8   12.9 #>  9 Chifley            366000       1020  2011         -8   12.8 #> 10 Chifley            486000        990  2011         -8   13.1 #> # ℹ 3,429 more rows #> # ℹ 1 more variable: log_block_size_std <dbl>

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: now use rstanarm fit model using full Bayesian inference Stan. assume effects log-transformed block size (shifted) year log-transformed UV vary division (.e. suburb). model , consider fixed random effect component overall intercept predictor coefficient estimates: fixed effects characterise effects common across divisions, random effects expressed deviations fixed effects vary across divisions. Written , model takes following form 𝚕𝚘𝚐_𝚄𝚅∼N(μ,σ)μ=μα+α[]+(μβ1+β1,[])𝚢𝚎𝚊𝚛_𝚜𝚒𝚗𝚌𝚎+(μβ2+β2,[])𝚕𝚘𝚐_𝚋𝚕𝚘𝚌𝚔_𝚜𝚒𝚣𝚎_𝚜𝚝𝚍 \begin{align} \mathtt{log\_UV} &\sim N\left(\mu, \sigma\right)\\ \mu &= \mu_\alpha + \alpha_{[]} + \left(\mu_{\beta_1} + \beta_{1, []} \right) \mathtt{year\_since} + \left(\mu_{\beta_2} + \beta_{2,[]}\right) \mathtt{log\_block\_size\_std}\\ \end{align} 𝚢𝚎𝚊𝚛_𝚜𝚒𝚗𝚌𝚎=𝚢𝚎𝚊𝚛−2019,𝚕𝚘𝚐_𝚋𝚕𝚘𝚌𝚔_𝚜𝚒𝚣𝚎_𝚜𝚝𝚍=log(𝚋𝚕𝚘𝚌𝚔_𝚜𝚒𝚣𝚎)−log(850). \begin{align} \mathtt{year\_since} &= \mathtt{year} - 2019\,,\\ \mathtt{log\_block\_size\_std} &= \log{(\mathtt{block\_size})} - \log{(850)}\,. \end{align}  model motivated assumption estimates characterising change log-transformed UV expected fixed component random suburb-dependent component. words division-level estimates expected normally distributed around mean value, .e. fixed effect. Using division random (rather fixed) effect allows partial pooling observations across divisions estimating division-level effects. reason particular variable transformations summarised following bullet points: year_since shifted year year_since = 0 corresponds 2019. words, 2019 arbitrarily chosen reference year. log_block_size_std log-transformed shifted block size; log-transform values since block size become negative; shifting log-transformed values means log_block_size_std = 0 corresponds block size 850 m2m^2, roughly median block size value across past sale records. Chapter 12 Gelman, Hill Vehtari, Regression Stories great discussion examples variable transformations motivate . fit model using standard lmer/lme4-syntax Stan using rstanarm::stan_glmer().
code: model <- stan_glmer(     log_UV ~ 1 + year_since + log_block_size_std + (1 + year_since + log_block_size_std | division),     data = data_model)

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: inspect pairwise correlations fixed-effect parameter estimates bivariate scatter plots. useful identifying divergencies, collinearities multiplicative non-identifiabilities.  note divergent transitions, Gaussian blobs/clouds suggest major issues estimates (see Visual MCMC diagnostics using bayesplot package lot details MCMC diagnostics). Next, compare samples drawn posterior predictive distribution 𝚕𝚘𝚐_𝚄𝚅\mathtt{log\_UV} values, show distribution leave-one-(LOO)-adjusted R-squared values Rs2R^2_s.  posterior predictive check suggests issues model fit. note, actual 𝚕𝚘𝚐_𝚄𝚅\mathtt{log\_UV} distribution shows small bump values logUV>14\log{\mathrm{UV}} > 14 (corresponding values greater $1.2 million) reproduced model. Instead large UV values pull mean posterior predictive slightly larger value actual distribution. median Bayesian Rs2R^2_s value 0.53 also indicates great fit; words: lot unexplained (residual) variance.
code: bayesplot_theme_set(theme_minimal()) color_scheme_set(scheme = c(pal, pal)) pairs(     model,      pars = c("(Intercept)", "year_since", "log_block_size_std", "sigma"),     transformations = list(sigma = "log")) ppc_dens_overlay(data_model$log_UV, posterior_predict(model)) +     labs(         x = "log(UV)",         y = "density",         title = "Posterior predictive check") loo_r2 <- loo_R2(model) #> Warning: Some Pareto k diagnostic values are too high. See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details. loo_r2 %>%     enframe() %>%     ggplot(aes(value)) +     geom_density() +      theme_minimal() +     labs(         x = "R²",         y = "density",         title = "Bayesian R² distribution")

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: show interpret fixed random-effect parameter estimates. broom.mixed::tidy() makes easy extract estimates standardised format.

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: show fixed parameter estimates (mean standard deviation) including 95% uncertainty estimates (based 2.5% 97.5% quantiles marginal posteriors). intercept estimate μα\mu_\alpha = 13.2. means estimated fixed-effect UV 2019 block size 850 m2\mathrm{m}^2 approximately exp(μα)\exp{(\mu_\alpha)} = $562.9k. coefficient estimate year_since μβ1\mu_{\beta_1} = 0.0211 means fixed-effect UV block size 850 m2\mathrm{m}^2 increases every year factor exp(μβ1)\exp{(\mu_{\beta_1})} = 1.02, .e. around 2%. coefficient estimate log_block_size_std μβ2\mu_{\beta_2} = 0.442 means 10% increase block size 2019 translated exp(μβ2log(1.1))\exp{(\mu_{\beta_2} \log(1.1))} = 1.04 factor increase UV, .e. 4% increase (UV). can compare fixed-effect estimates mixed-effect model complete pooling model, .e. model ignore division-level differences. note wider uncertainty intervals fixed-effect estimates mixed-effect model, probably realistic given variability division-level effects.
code: effect_fixed <- broom.mixed::tidy(model, "fixed", conf.int = TRUE) effect_fixed #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   term               estimate std.error conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>                 <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 (Intercept)         13.2      0.0677   13.1      13.4    #> 2 year_since           0.0211   0.00178   0.0179    0.0243 #> 3 log_block_size_std   0.442    0.0573    0.345     0.536 model_complete_pooling <- stan_glm(     log_UV ~ 1 + year_since + log_block_size_std,     data = data_model) broom::tidy(model_complete_pooling, "fixed", conf.int = TRUE)

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: Random-effect estimates shown standard deviations underlying (normal) distributions correlation coefficients covariance matrix. following table summarises estimates including residual standard deviation σ\sigma. standard deviation estimate random-effect intercept distribution sd(α[])\mathrm{sd}(\alpha_{[]}) = 0.241 means 68% properties (2019 block size 850 m2\mathrm{m}^2) across divisions UV range [μα−sd(α[]),μα+sd(α[])][\mu_\alpha - \mathrm{sd}(\alpha_{[]}), \mu_\alpha + \mathrm{sd}(\alpha_{[]})] = [$442.5k, $716.2k]. standard deviation estimate random-effect component year_since effect sd(β1,[])\mathrm{sd}(\beta_{1,[]}) = 0.00457; means per-year UV increase 68% properties (block size 850 m2\mathrm{m}^2) across divisions range [μβ1−sd(β1,[]),μβ1+sd(β1,[])][\mu_{\beta_1} - \mathrm{sd}(\beta_{1,[]}), \mu_{\beta_1} + \mathrm{sd}(\beta_{1,[]})] = [1.7%, 2.6%]. standard deviation estimate random-effect component log_block_size_std effect sd(β2,[])\mathrm{sd}(\beta_{2,[]}) = 0.186; means 10% increase block size 68% properties across divisions 2019 translated UV increase range [2.5%, 6.2%].
code: effect_random <- broom.mixed::tidy(model, "ran_pars", conf.int = TRUE) effect_random #> # A tibble: 7 × 3 #>   term                                        group     estimate #>   <chr>                                       <chr>        <dbl> #> 1 sd_(Intercept).division                     division  0.241    #> 2 sd_year_since.division                      division  0.00457  #> 3 sd_log_block_size_std.division              division  0.186    #> 4 cor_(Intercept).year_since.division         division  0.158    #> 5 cor_(Intercept).log_block_size_std.division division  0.000631 #> 6 cor_year_since.log_block_size_std.division  division -0.000164 #> 7 sd_Observation.Residual                     Residual  0.214

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: use model predict UV values across Woden valley suburbs function block size every 5 years 2010 2030 . easy using modelr::data_grid() create grid values used input model; tidybayes::add_predicted_draws() draws samples posterior predictive distribution conditional generated input data.
code: data_pred <- data_model %>%     data_grid(         division = unique(division),         year_since = seq(2010L, 2030L, by = 5) - 2019L,         log_block_size_std = log(seq(100, 2000, by = 10)) - log(850)) %>%     add_predicted_draws(model) %>%     group_by(division, year_since, log_block_size_std) %>%     median_qi() %>%     ungroup() %>%     mutate(         year = year_since + 2019L,         block_size = exp(log_block_size_std + log(850)))

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: now draw median 95% quantile intervals UV predictions function block size every division (suburb) year. use plotly produce interactive visualisation, details given mouse-hover.
code:
# Create nested data data_pl <- data_pred %>% group_by(division) %>% nest() %>% ungroup() # Silence warnings that originate from using `frame` options(warn = -1) # Create plotly subplots map2( data_pl$division, data_pl$data, function(suburb, df) { df %>% plot_ly( x = ~block_size, frame = ~year, customdata = ~ sprintf( "%s, %i m²
Median UV: A$ %ik
95%% CI [%ik, %ik]", suburb, round(block_size), round(exp(.prediction) / 1e3), round(exp(.lower) / 1e3), round(exp(.upper) / 1e3)), height = 1000) %>% add_lines( y = ~exp(.prediction), line = list(color = pal[1]), showlegend = FALSE, hovertemplate = "%{customdata}") %>% add_ribbons( ymin = ~exp(.lower), ymax = ~exp(.upper), color = NA, fillcolor = pal[1], opacity = 0.2, showlegend = FALSE, hoverinfo = "none") %>% layout( yaxis = list( title = "", range = c(0, max(exp(df$.upper)))), xaxis = list(title = ""), annotations = list(list( x = 0.5, y = 1, text = suburb, xref = "paper", yref = "paper", xanchor = "center", yanchor = "bottom", showarrow = FALSE)) ) }) %>% subplot( nrows = 5, shareX = TRUE) %>% layout( margin = list(t = 50, r = 10, t = 10, b = 10), xaxis = list(title = "Block size [m²]"), yaxis = list(title = "Unimproved Value (UV) [A$]")) %>% animation_button(visible = FALSE)

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: also show static version years 2010, 2020 2030, include recorded UV data 2020 sales.
code: # Reset warning level to default options(warn = 0) data_pred %>%     filter(year %in% c(2010, 2020, 2030)) %>%     mutate(year = as.factor(year)) %>%     ggplot(aes(block_size, exp(.prediction), colour = year, fill = year)) +     geom_point(         data = data_model %>% filter(year == 2020L),          aes(x = block_size, y = unimproved_value),         colour = pal[2],         inherit.aes = FALSE) +     geom_line() +      geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = exp(.lower), ymax = exp(.upper)), colour = NA, alpha = 0.2) +     facet_wrap(~ division, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +     scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +     scale_colour_manual(values = pal) +     scale_y_continuous(         labels=scales::dollar_format(accuracy = 1, scale = 1e-3, suffix = "k")) +     theme_minimal() +     labs(         x = "Block size [m²]", y = "Unimproved Value (UV) [A$]",          fill = "Year", colour = "Year") +     theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Modelling the unimproved value in the ACT's Woden Valley
text: note observations: Properties block size 1000 m2m^2 highest UV suburbs Phillip, Swinger Hill Garran 2030. Lyons, Mawson Torrens properties size predicted lowest UV om 2030. suburbs Phillip, Swinger Hill Hughes predicted show largest change UV 2030 relative 2025. suburbs Lyons, Mawson, Pearce predicted show smallest change UV period.

title: Variability of the unimproved value per sqm across the ACT
text: goal case study show combine functionalities range external packages gain insight variability unimproved value (UV) based Allhomes past sales ACT. Key packages allhomes extracting Allhomes past sales data, strayr getting ABS geometries SA2 regions (– within cities – usually represent suburbs), leaflet drawing interactive Leaflet maps R.

title: Variability of the unimproved value per sqm across the ACT
text: load necessary non-base R libraries.
code: library(allhomes) library(tidyverse) library(strayr)    # Install with `remotes::install_github("runapp-aus/strayr")` library(sf) library(leaflet) library(htmltools)

title: Variability of the unimproved value per sqm across the ACT
text: obtain 2021 statistical area 2 (SA2) geospatial data provided ABS strayr, keep records areas ACT. SA2 names sometimes include territory name, clean names removing territory name present. store cleaned SA2 names preparation using names past sales allhomes search.
code: data_spatial <- read_absmap("sa22021") |>     filter(state_name_2021 == "Australian Capital Territory") |>     mutate(sa2_name_2021 = str_remove_all(sa2_name_2021, "\\s\\(ACT\\).*$")) sa2_names <- data_spatial |>      pull(sa2_name_2021) %>%      unique() |>      sort() |>     str_subset("-|No usual", negate = TRUE)

title: Variability of the unimproved value per sqm across the ACT
text: now get past sales data suburbs given sa2_names years 2021 2022. Since get_past_sale_data() requires suburbs specified format “suburb_name, state/territory_abbreviation”, append ", ACT" entries sa2_names. process may take minutes.
code: data_allhomes <- sa2_names |> paste0(", ACT") |>      map_dfr(function(burb)          map_dfr(2021L:2022L, function(yr) get_past_sales_data(burb, yr))) #> Warning in get_past_sales_data(burb, yr): No sales data for suburb 'Acton, ACT' #> Warning in validate_suburb(suburb): Could not validate suburb 'Arboretum, ACT' #> Warning in get_past_sales_data(burb, yr): No sales data for suburb title: Variability of the unimproved value per sqm across the ACT
text: now combine geospatial Allhomes past sales data; use inner-join filter suburbs without UV data, summarise UV data per property sale give median well upper lower 95% quantile band values UV per square metre every suburb. Prior aggregating UV data keep entries block size 100 2000 sqm unimproved value exceeds $100; filter large scale commercial sales zero-UV-value outliers. can now visualise median UV values per sqm every suburb Leaflet map. 95% quantile band median values every suburb detailed mouse hover.
code:
data <- data_spatial |> inner_join( data_allhomes |> filter(between(block_size, 100, 2000), unimproved_value > 100) |> mutate(UV_per_sqm = unimproved_value / block_size) |> reframe( UV_per_sqm = quantile( UV_per_sqm, probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), na.rm = TRUE), quant = c("l", "m", "h"), .by = division) |> pivot_wider(names_from = "quant", values_from = "UV_per_sqm"), by = c("sa2_name_2021" = "division")) pal <- colorNumeric("YlOrRd", domain = data$m) leaflet(data = data, height = 1000) %>% addTiles() %>% addPolygons( fillColor = ~pal(m), fillOpacity = 0.7, color = "white", weight = 1, smoothFactor = 0.2, highlightOptions = highlightOptions( weight = 5, color = "#666", fillOpacity = 0.7, bringToFront = TRUE), label = sprintf( "%s
UV per m²: %s
95%% CI: [%s, %s]", data$sa2_name_2021, sprintf("$%.0f", data$m), sprintf("$%.0f", data$l), sprintf("$%.0f", data$h)) %>% map(HTML), labelOptions = labelOptions( style = list("font-weight" = "normal", padding = "3px 8px"), textsize = "15px", direction = "auto")) %>% addLegend( pal = pal, values = ~m, opacity = 0.7, title = "Unimproved Value (UV) per m²", position = "bottomright")

title: Extract Past Sales Data from Allhomes.com.au
text: goal allhomes extract past sales data specific suburb(s) year(s) Australian property website allhomes.com.au. Allhomes data include address property details, date price sale, block size unimproved value properties mainly ACT NSW.

title: Extract Past Sales Data from Allhomes.com.au
text: Changes Allhomes website required data extraction method shift scraping (static) HTML pages using Allhomes’ GraphQL API. change leverages website’s dynamic data loading via GraphQL endpoint (/graphql), provides structured JSON responses sales history data. Key improvements include: Direct API Access: Instead parsing rendered HTML, package now queries GraphQL API persisted queries variables (e.g., locality slug, filters, pagination). Reliability: GraphQL allows consistent data retrieval, reducing risk breakage website layout changes. However, reliability depends SHA256 hash persisted queries changing; server-side hash changes, requests fail. Efficiency: Paginated data fetched directly, avoiding need scrape multiple HTML pages. technical details GraphQL implementation, refer internal documentation (allhomes_graphql_scraping_brief.md).

title: Extract Past Sales Data from Allhomes.com.au
text: allhomes also provides two datasets: divisions_ACT: Division names IDs ACT. divisions_NSW: Division names IDs NSW.

what: Extract historical Allhomes past sales data for an ACT/NSW suburb. — get_past_sales_data
title: Extract historical Allhomes past sales data for an ACT/NSW suburb. — get_past_sales_data
text: Extract historical Allhomes past sales data ACT/NSW suburb.

title: Extract historical Allhomes past sales data for an ACT/NSW suburb. — get_past_sales_data
code: get_past_sales_data(suburb, year, max_entries = 5000)

title: Extract historical Allhomes past sales data for an ACT/NSW suburb. — get_past_sales_data
text: suburb character scalar denoting specific suburb. Format must "<suburb_name>, <state/territory_abbreviation>", e.g. "Balmain, ACT". year integer scalar denoting year. NULL, historical data suburb returned. Otherwise sales data indicated year returned. max_entries maximum number records returned. Defaults 5000.

text: tibble.
